title: Japan’s Rapidus Takes on TSMC With Aggressive 2nm Pricing
gist: Japan's Rapidus is targeting 2nm mass production in 2027 with wafer prices of ¥3M-3.5M (~$18,460-$21,540), undercutting TSMC's reported ~$30,000 2nm wafer price, while TSMC and Samsung are simultaneously raising prices on legacy advanced nodes amid tight capacity.

insights.claim: Rapidus, targeting 2nm mass production in 2027, plans to price its foundry services at levels comparable to or slightly lower than TSMC as it seeks to gain a market foothold, per CEO Atsuyoshi Koike.
insights.speaker: M
insights.so_what: A credible low-cost 2nm challenger to TSMC would compress advanced-node foundry margins industry-wide and give AI/HPC chip customers a negotiating lever against TSMC price hikes.
insights.quote: Rapidus, which is targeting 2nm mass production in 2027, plans to price its foundry services at levels comparable to or lower than those of industry leader TSMC as it seeks to gain a foothold in the market.
insights.locator: The race for advanced nodes among foundries

insights.claim: Rapidus is targeting a reference wafer price of ¥3 million to ¥3.5 million (approximately $18,460 to $21,540 at current exchange rates), though the final price could fluctuate with exchange rates.
insights.speaker: M
insights.so_what: Gives investors a concrete price anchor to compare against TSMC's and Samsung's 2nm wafer pricing when assessing foundry competitive dynamics.
insights.quote: Rapidus is targeting a reference price of ¥3 million to ¥3.5 million per wafer, although the final price could fluctuate depending on factors such as exchange rates, the report adds.
insights.locator: Nikkei, citing Rapidus CEO Atsuyoshi Koike

insights.claim: Rapidus is holding customer discussions with more than 60 companies, the majority overseas, while also targeting 1.4nm production in 2029 and building a second fab in Chitose, Hokkaido for future capacity expansion.
insights.speaker: M
insights.so_what: 60+ prospective customers, mostly overseas, suggests Rapidus is trying to break into the same global fabless customer base TSMC serves, a leading indicator of real competitive threat versus government-subsidized vanity project.
insights.quote: Rapidus is accelerating customer engagement efforts, holding discussions with more than 60 companies, the majority of which are overseas customers

insights.claim: TSMC's 2nm process and Intel's 18A (1.8nm-class) node are reportedly priced at around $30,000 per wafer, significantly above Rapidus' targeted pricing of up to roughly $21,000 per wafer.
insights.speaker: M
insights.so_what: A roughly 30-40% pricing gap versus TSMC/Intel at leading-edge nodes, if real orders materialize, could pressure TSMC's advanced-node pricing power and margin structure.
insights.quote: TSMC’s 2nm process and Intel’s 18A (1.8nm-class) node are reportedly priced at around US$30,000 per wafer. If accurate, the pricing would be significantly above Rapidus’ targeted pricing of up to US$21,000 per wafer.
insights.locator: 2nm Pricing Battle Intensifies Among Foundry Giants

insights.claim: Samsung Electronics' 2nm process is estimated at approximately $20,000 per wafer, a more aggressive pricing stance than TSMC as Samsung seeks to attract advanced-node customers.
insights.speaker: M
insights.so_what: Samsung's $20,000 pricing is already close to Rapidus' target range, meaning Rapidus isn't undercutting the whole market — just TSMC — narrowing its true differentiation to TSMC-specific customers.
insights.quote: Samsung Electronics’ 2nm process is estimated at approximately US$20,000 per wafer, highlighting its more aggressive pricing stance as it seeks to attract advanced-node customers

insights.claim: TSMC has informed major customers including Nvidia, Apple, and AMD of plans to raise wafer prices by 5% to 10% for advanced processes such as 3nm, 5nm, and the widely used 7nm node.
insights.speaker: M
insights.so_what: A 5-10% price hike across TSMC's core advanced nodes directly raises input costs for Nvidia, Apple, and AMD chip production, a margin headwind investors should model into those companies' COGS.
insights.quote: TSMC has recently informed major customers, including NVIDIA, Apple, and AMD, of plans to raise wafer prices by 5% to 10% for advanced processes such as 3nm and 5nm, as well as the 7nm node
insights.locator: Industry sources cited by Chosun Biz

insights.claim: Samsung is raising supply prices for new customers by around 15%, mainly for high-demand advanced nodes such as 4nm and 5nm, as well as selected 8nm automotive nodes, though this is viewed as targeted price normalization rather than a broad increase like TSMC's.
insights.speaker: M
insights.so_what: A steeper 15% hike at Samsung versus TSMC's 5-10% suggests Samsung is repricing from a previously discounted position, while TSMC's broader increase signals structural pricing power from persistent capacity tightness.
insights.quote: Samsung is raising supply prices for new customers by around 15%, mainly for high-demand advanced nodes such as 4nm and 5nm, as well as selected 8nm nodes used for automotive semiconductors.
insights.locator: A similar trend is emerging at Samsung’s

insights.claim: Both TSMC and Samsung are raising advanced-node prices amid surging AI-driven demand and tight capacity, even as Rapidus attempts to undercut them on price to gain a market foothold.
insights.speaker: M
insights.so_what: The simultaneous price hikes by incumbents and undercutting by Rapidus frames a bifurcating foundry market: capacity-constrained leaders extracting more margin from AI demand while a subsidized new entrant tries to buy share on price.
insights.quote: Nonetheless, both foundry giants are said to be raising prices amid surging demand and tight capacity.
